note_data.title: Chords and Arcs
keyPoints: A chord is a line segment that joins any two points on a circle
An arc is a part of the circumference of a circle between two points
Equal chords subtend equal arcs in the same circle or in congruent circles
The perpendicular from the center of a circle to a chord bisects the chord
Equal chords are equidistant from the center of the circle
The angle subtended by an arc at the center is double the angle subtended by the same arc at any point on the circle
Chords equidistant from the center are equal in length
The perpendicular bisector of a chord passes through the center of the circle

definitions.definition: A chord of a circle is a line segment that joins any two points on the circle.
An arc is a part of the circumference of a circle between two points on the circle.
The longer arc between two points on a circle is called the major arc.
The shorter arc between two points on a circle is called the minor arc.
The angle subtended by an arc at the center of the circle is called the central angle.

importantQuestions.question: Prove that equal chords of a circle are equidistant from the center.
importantQuestions.answer: Let AB and CD be two equal chords. Draw perpendiculars OM and ON from center O. In right triangles OMA and ONC, OA = OC (radii), AM = CN (half of equal chords), therefore OM = ON by Pythagoras theorem.

importantQuestions.question: Prove that the perpendicular from the center of a circle to a chord bisects the chord.
importantQuestions.answer: Given: Circle with center O, chord AB, OM ⊥ AB. To Prove: AM = MB. Proof: In triangles OMA and OMB, OA = OB (radii), OM = OM (common), ∠OMA = ∠OMB = 90°. Therefore triangles are congruent by RHS. Hence AM = MB by CPCT.

mcqs.question: If two chords of a circle are equal, then they are:
The angle subtended by a semicircle at any point on the circle is:
If the perpendicular distance from center to a chord is zero, then the chord is:

commonMistakes: Students confuse major arc with minor arc when not specified clearly
Forgetting to state that chords must be in the same circle or congruent circles
Not drawing perpendicular from center properly in chord problems
Mixing up the relationship between central angle and inscribed angle
Not using the property that perpendicular from center bisects the chord
examTips: This chapter usually has 1-2 MCQs and 1 short question worth 3-4 marks in BSEK papers
Always draw clear diagrams - perpendicular from center to chord appears every year
Memorize the exact wordings of theorems as they are often asked for definitions
Practice numerical problems involving Pythagoras theorem with chord distances
formulas: If d is distance from center to chord of length 2l, then d² + l² = r² (where r is radius)
Central angle = 2 × Inscribed angle (for same arc)
note_data.keyDiagram: Circle with center O, chord AB, perpendicular OM from center to chord meeting at M (midpoint). Show radii OA and OB. This diagram must be drawn for most chord-related proofs.
